Ding et al., 2020; Du et al., 2020; Geng et al., 2024b; Hosseini et al., 2023b; Ju et al., 2023c; M. J. Khan et al., 2024; Kim et al., 2024d; Maheshwari et al., 2022; Padala et al., 2021b; C. Pan et al., 2024; Pentyala et al., 2022b; Rodríguez-Gálvez et al., 2021b; Salem et al., 2022; K. Sun, 2025c; Vijay, 2025b; J. Xu et al., 2021; Yaghini et al., 2023b; H. Yan et al., 2023b; P. Zhang & Liu, 2025; R. Zhao et al., 2024b)

In this part, we will discuss about how we did the experiment and the methodology we used to perform the experiment to find the optimal point of trade-off between Accuracy and privacy in mobile recommendation system of movies. We will discuss about the approaches, methods tools that we utilised and procedures to investigate and research about or core research questions. The methodology that we are employing addresses and inquires accuracy-privacy-fairness, its trade-offs which is found and is quite prevalent in federated systems employed in mobile devices for recommendation of movies to the users. What we are most interested is to enquire the selection and processing of datasets to simulate various mobile environments in different types of devices. The devices are especially geared up to simulate resource-constrained real and physical devices; including the configuration of FL protocols which account for non-IID data distributions. Then we also discuss and employ privacy preservation mechanisms such as privacy and secure aggregation to compute the metrics.

In this part, we explain how we selected the dataset, what the dataset actually contains, and also what kind of preprocessing and data partitioning strategies we used so that we can simulate a real federated learning (FL) setting for a mobile movie recommendation system.

For this work we mainly use the MovieLens-1M dataset, which contains around 1,000,209 explicit ratings, and we use it because it helps us to realistically simulate personalization and user heterogeneity. This dataset is also very commonly used in FL recommender system research because it is quite sparse (around ~95%), it has diverse user behaviour patterns, and it is closely related to the mobile streaming domain, where ratings can be treated similar to watch-history, likes, or content requests.

For the preprocessing step, what we do first is we filter the data a bit. Basically, we remove users and movies that have less than 5 interactions, because this way we can reduce the sparsity of the dataset and also handle the cold-start issue to some extent, while still keeping the interactions that are actually meaningful for our experiment. After that, we convert the explicit 1–5 ratings into implicit feedback by doing binarization, where any rating that is ≥ 4 is treated as a positive interaction as 1 and the rest is treated as 0. We do this mainly because in real mobile app cases we usually don’t have full rating values all the time, but more like simple signals such as “watched”, “liked”, or “clicked”, and the missing ratings are treated like unobserved interactions. We also use the timestamp information to do a chronological train-test split (80/20), since it is more realistic for sequential recommendation where the model learns from past behaviour and then predicts future behaviour. On top of this, we apply per-user normalization to standardize the ratings, so that differences caused by users rating in different ways (like some users always rate high and some rate low) is reduced. To simulate federated learning (FL) in a realistic way across different types of mobile clients, we split the data horizontally (so, user-based partitioning). What we do is randomly assign users to K = 100 clients, and then each client only keeps the interactions that belong to the users assigned to it. This setup naturally creates a non-IID data distribution, because users behave differently (different preferences, different activity levels, and also different amounts of data, which often follows a power-law type pattern). We also make sure the client dataset sizes are not all the same (for example, roughly around 10–200 users per client) so that it better reflects the heterogeneity we usually see in real mobile settings, and even after processing there is still around ~45–80% sparsity left. With this overall setup, we can preserve privacy (since the raw user data never leaves the client), keep things scalable for edge/mobile devices, and more properly evaluate the accuracy–privacy trade-off under realistic non-IID federated learning conditions.

Describes the models of machine learning implemented (matrix factorization) and their mathematical formulation and implementation decisions in order to make them amenable to mobile deployment. This entails adaptations to exceed the resource constrained environments by means of quantization and model pruning by taking into consideration efficient inference in a variety of mobile hardware. Furthermore, in this section, we'll talk on how these models can be

particularly designed for recommender systems, like collaborative filtering and the matrix factorization approaches to guarantee high relevant and high performance to the recommender system in the federated learning paradigm. This also includes combination of novel architectures dedicated for the federated scenarios such as federated collaborative filtering or federated matrix factorizational decentralized training and privacy preservation. The mathematical formulations of such models will be presented which hereby underline the role that crucial models play in enabling privacy-preserving mechanisms, e.g. secure aggregation or privacy that do not come at cost for model utility. The choices and the adaptation of such models are important for ensuring that the proposed federated learning framework is computationally feasible on mobile devices, and can capture complex user-item interactions for purposes of accurately recommending movies. These architectural decisions are done carefully under consideration of strength against dictionaries that isn't meet IID data distribution assumption and ability to maintain their performance under different communication constraint which is natural to mobile FL environments. In addition, the methodologies include the accommodation on the client side whereby individual devices can optimize global models using their own local data and this can be made to allow the personalization to better accuracy and privacy boundaries. The rationale of the multi-global model strategy that is embedded in a hierarchical framework to work with heterogeneous in device and data distributions will also be discussed, and will improve the scalability and personalization from end to end across the network. This approach makes personalized and efficient learning for diversity devices and data distributions possible where the approach means time departing from traditional FL paradigms also by the realization of multi-global model approach in hierarchical framework. This multi-global model strategy is in contrast to conventional federated learning strategies that usually maintain a single global model, in which instead of depending on different client characteristics and preferences, they have more nuanced approach to take care of the diversity of the data. This enables a more adaptive and resilient system which is useful in context where there is huge heterogeneity between users, and between different device capabilities.

Describes the federated learning protocol (FedAvg) and the communication mechanism and training procedures and the way the clients are involved in the distributed training procedure. This section will describe the FedAvg algorithm as base algorithm and dedicated algorithms to deal with the non-IID data using multi-global algorithms in a 3-tier federated learning architecture. This hierarchical framework has attained better data privacy, computation efficiency and scalability, compared with the traditional client-server FL models. In particular, there are three layered architecture to include clients, edge layer and federated edge layer, which make efficient aggregation and personalized management of models. This architecture allows for a solid and robust approach to handle the non-IID data characteristics by aggregation across multiple layer and supports for multiple global models leading to a significant increase in the efficacy of the model and addressing the data & computation heterogeneity. As the significant coordinator the edge layer explores the metadata of the results obtained from training sessions of clients to give information on model states and requirements as well as to query the federated edge layer in case a suitable model can not be found locally. This multi-tiered approach is more granular in terms of aggregation and distribution of models and more adaptive to the fluid and varied nature of distribution of movie recommendations to the mobile medium. The fedge layer in turn is in charge of the storing and assuring of currency and representativness of these different global models with respect to various data landscape. This kind of structural orchestration allows well selected model and distribution strategy which is especially beneficial for case scenarios using different mobile clients with different data distributions and different computational capacities in movie recommendation system. This holistic solution provides for both the global awareness and individual preferences to be well represented and user privacy and efficiency to be kept. The

information exchange between the clients and the server is based on well-known algorithms (e.g. FedAvg) however, the possibility to add custom aggregation strategies to adapt to a specific research need. The server is then used to aggregate these local updates so that it can be used to refine the global model and disseminate the improved version back to clients in order to conduct further training rounds until convergence takes place. This is an iterative process which is the main component of federated learning and thus only allow federated learning to train powerful models in a non-centralised manner, hence improving privacy. This distributed method of working helps ensure that security-sensitive data is not stored on the company servers but on individual devices and is aware of the strict data protection laws and user expectations of user privacy.

Describes the implementation of the addition of privacy using DP-SGD (step by step), i.e.: Gradient clipping, Noise addition and accounting for privacy budget using Renyi Privacy Implementation In addition, the combination of other techniques including gradient sparsification and coding will be considered for the sparse communication updates of the local models, such that only significant updates which are in the parameter space will be efficiently transmitted. This layering of defense mechanism provides a significant improvement of privacy on higher guarantees than basic DP, in terms of resiliency in the system against various kinds of inferrence attacks, and how efficient the resources being optimized. These in turn are important strategies to help balance the rigid requirements of protecting people's privacy and achieving acceptable model performance, in the case of resource constrained mobile environments. In addition, the proposed approach integrates the local privacy mechanisms into the training pipeline directly in order to further improve the privacy protection. This is done by adding precisely calibrated noise on the client updates before transmitting them to the server, thus both individual contributions will be indistinguishable but not stop them from contributing to the learning of the model. This mechanism, mostly implemented by DP-SGD involving the use of per sample gradient clipping to restrain the sensitivity of single updates and then addition of calibrated gaussian noise from distribution scaled to the privacy budget (kept track of using renyi privacy for the tight composition bounds) assures that the aggregated global model encapsulates these overarching patterns in the federation without, during the aggregation phase at the central server, memorizing and over fitting any private data of any given user. Gradient clipping clipping the L2-norm of the gradient of each sample that prevents any one client's update transitivity and unnecessarily impacting on the global model, and the noise injection makes the individual contributions statistically indistinguishable from each other or random perturbations and so formally guarantees of , - privacy bound the privacy leakage, no matter what the adversary's access to the model's outputs or model intermediates. This is not only useful for warding off sophisticated threats of inference such as membership inference or reconstruction attacks (where the adversaries may be able to infer user-specific preferences over movies from the gradient patterns), but it also is empirically useful for improving model generalisation, acting as a form of regularisation, as we will see when we touch on the bigger question of large-scale federated training scenarios where DP-FedAvg leads to production-ready models (immune to unintended memorisation of rare or sensitive user-item interactions).

Articulates the adversarial evaluative methods for testing for effectiveness of privacy protection mechanisms. This comprises simulating prominent attacks such membership inference attacks, attribute inference attacks, data reconstruction attacks, model poisoning attacks as observed specific for federated recommender systems in case of movie recommendations with non-IID data distributions. Membership inference attacks, for example, permit a server that is honest, but curious about, the fact that the behavior in this case of the server model and/or corresponding gradients are different according to the synthetic data that were used to train the models in

shadow settings of happy but curious data providers and to infer some specific interactions between users and items. Attribute inference Attacks piggyback on uploaded model parameters and thereby infer sensitive user demographics or preferences, and even in a privacy-equipped federated setup find vulnerabilities. Reconstruction attacks by the server Reconstructing private ratings or interactions from the gradients. >80% accuracy in matrix factorization cases on data such as MovieLens. Reconstruction attacks from the server point to the risks of hierarchical FL architectures. Poisoning attacks, which is design as model poisoning or gradient poisoning attacks is a series of attacks where an adversary will make use of a small number of malicious clients in order to simulate the behavior of a synthetic of fake users to upload carefully crafted gradients in an abuse of the federated aggregation rounds. These adverse updates give the global model parameter updates, especially item embeddings, so as to artificially increase the visibility and ranking of some target movies in top-

recommendations, without any prior knowledge about benign clients' data distributions or interactions. For example to increase their attacks, attacker approximate benign user embeddings by using random sampling or hard mining techniques, and create a poisoned gradients to push the representation of target item in the embedding space is like popular movie by inherent popularities in recommender systems. Such attacks are particularly powerful in non-IID situations similar to movie recommendation datasets like MovieLens where it only take 1-5% of all clients to be malicious, and are able to increase hit rate and exposure of unpopular target movies by significant 20-50%, across users, with no negative effect on the overall model accuracy, and without any negative effect on the overall accuracy. These evaluations are helpful to quantify leakage of privacy and integrity using standardized measurement criteria, e.g. attack success rate (e.g. target item promotion rate (in top-k lists)), F1-scores in terms of discriminability of malicious influences, re-construction fidelity (in terms of recoverab

e private interactions), model utility degradation (e.g. NDCG drop). Empirical results confirm the resiliency a number of proposed defenses like DP-SGD which adds crafted perturbations by adding calibrated Gaussian noise so that are indistinguishable from normal updates, gradient sparsification which sends only parameter changes with significant magnitude to the dilution malicious parameter changes impacts, and multi-global model strategies of which diversifies the aggregation over heterogeous client clusters that reduces the efficacy of such attacks with 30-70% limited accuracy reduction. Such comprehensive evaluations are important in order to discover more about the boundaries of the security of federated recommender systems in practice, and especially, in real world scenarios, in a mobile environment where user bases are diverse and potentially adversarial.

Includes specifics of measuring important computational resources of crucial interest to practical implementation of federated recommender systems on resource constrained mobile devices, e.g., CPU usage, memory usage, energy consumption, communication overhead and inference time. These metrics are profiled by using special tools like I-Prof for predicting computation time and energy impact of local training tasks on android devices, Trepn and Snapdragon Profiler for monitoring power consumption of computation at a whole system level, benchmarks like FedScale which uses real world device traces for computation speed (FLOPs/s), communication throughput (MB/s) and client availability patterns. In federated movie recommendation scenarios, with large item catalogs (for example using MovieLens, containing e.g. thousands of movies), the limited memory consumption is one of the main bottlenecks.128 dimensional item embedding has a huge memory footprint (10.2 KB), which makes it not feasible to fully store millions of items (>1GB) in typical smart phones with 4-8 GB total RAM, mainly requiring solutions such as elastic item embeddings or server-side storing of items, to limit the memory consumption of each client to 25-512MB. Computation is measured in terms of FLOPs or time per local epoch. 1-5 seconds per round on normal CPUs(more precisely 4x vCPU) are acceptable so that user can not see the too much waiting times. Energy profiling aim at <0.1% FL participation on a daily basis, for which a

combination of limited continuous FL participation to <0.1% of cases (e.g. energy consumption) with adequate privacy guarantees (e.g. DP-SGD - Gaussian noise computation overhead) and regularization benefit are provided by e.g., FLeet (0.036% per day). These communication metrics are aimed at tracking gradient size uploads (e.g. 1-10MB/round in the case of embedding-heavy models) against mobile data budgets (< 100MB/hour) in favour of compression (e.g. sparsification). Latency, for on-device inference, have to be less than 200ms (top-K recommendations) for IoT-like devices (64MB RAM); and less than 50-200ms for cellphones (512MB RAM) so that the systems can be used in real-time. in a non-IID, These are thresholds to make 'acceptable' deployment: e.g. peak memory <20% device total < energy < 1mJ update round time <10s Who validated empirically to remain model to useful without too much churn Comprehensive profiling on heterogeneous fleets to measure trade offs e.g. participant selection, model pruning, for production-scale federated movie recommenders.

In order to assess the quality of recommendations provided by our federated learning system, we use a set of standard evaluation metrics that are commonly used in recommender systems research, and that correspond to various aspects of model quality such as ranking quality, retrieval effectiveness, prediction quality and user coverage. Key ranking metrics include Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG@10) which provides a higher weight to the relevant items found closer to the top of the list (which is the reward) through position-based discounting, Hit@10 or Hit Rate@10 which measures the proportion of users for whom at least one relevant item is present in the top-10 recommendations, Precision@10 which measures the ratio of top-10 items that are relevant, Recall@10 which measures the ratio of all relevant items retrieved in the top-10 recommendations (Jadon & Patil, 20

These metrics are computed on a held out test set (20% of the dataset) separated temporally from the training data by chronological splits (e.g. 80% oldest interactions for the train dataset), as is common in recommender systems testing. This approach is emulating real-world deployment in the sense that future interactions cannot leak into the prediction as well as allowing to robustly assess generalization to unseen user-item interactions which is important for federated problems with stochasticity as well as heterogeneity

Together with the next section which is Experimental Setup, the above components of the Federated Movie Recommendation System, including System Architecture for Federated Movie Recommendation, Dataset Description and Preprocessing, Federated Learning Model Design, Privacy Mechanisms Implemented and Evaluation Metrics and Resource Profiling and Metrics would in large part ensure an extensive and holistic methodology for the fostering of privacy preserving Federated recommender systems.

The System Architecture illustrates a layered client-server architecture around the notion of movie recommendations, both for the decentralized process training, which is based on the user devices, and for global models aggregated at the server able to work with large size item catalogue as MovieLens avoiding memory constraint by dedicated strategies as using elastic embeddings or also with large items at the server . Dataset Description and Preprocessing Describes the treatment of the real world and large benchmarks, such as MovieLens, e.g. sparsity, cold start problems, non-IID distributions common in user interaction data, normalization, splitting strategies, synthetic data augmentation in order to simulate heterogeneous client behaviours . The Federated Learning Model Design, in which the embedding based architectures can be embedded with local personalization, such as matrix factorization, or graph neural networks, adapted to on physical computation is stated, to overcome statistic heterogeneity -in which HL user show skewed preferences and interaction frequencies.

Privacy Mechanisms Implemented Provide integrated defenses as, e. g. privacy through stochastic gradient descent, and gaussian noise injection of gradient sparsification reduce transmitted sensitive update and multi-global model aggregation across client clusters such as effectively defend the attribute inference attack of inferring demographics from model parameters, the reconstruction attack of recovering private ratings with over 80% accuracy, the poisoning attack of adversarial clients craft the gradient to boost target items by 20-50% in topk lists . Evaluation Metrics including the standardized components such as the hit rate , F1 tens for attack detection, the reconstruction fidelity, the model utility degradation, as well as accuracy as price privacy trade off measured with the privacy budgets ( epsilon ) and the utility drops of a model in an adversarial environment . Finally, Resource Profiling and Metrics does benchmarking of critical constraints on mobiles with programs and tools including I-Prof, Trepn, and FedScale traces addressing deadlines of critical resource constrictions including peak memory of 20% device RAM requirement (128 par 264 megabytes), energy less than 1mJ per update and 0.1% per day, communication in form of sparsified gradients, and inference latency less than 200 ms, viability in heterogeneous fleets, participant selection, and pruning tasks.

This frameworks include, in addition to the theoretical background. (e.g. FL convergence under non-IID data ), architectural designs. (e.g. horizontal vs. vertical FedRec paradigms ), practical optimisations. (e.g. adaptive aggregation for stragglers ) customised for privacy provisioning, resource limited FedRecs, comes with a direct answer to many of the challenges such as the amplifying impacts of non-IID data distributions on personalization disparities, the heterogeneity of adversarial attack scenarios (from poisoning through fake user or gradients attacks), as well as mobile deployment constraints. (e.g. system heterogeneity and high communication costs)

The Experimental Setup section implement this methodology in terms of details in simulation environment (e.g. FedDemo or Flower for multi-device emulation), hyperparameter setting (e.g. local epochs, learning rates optimized for non-IID), baseline models for benchmarking (e.g. FedAvg, FedProx, centralized MF) and strict evaluation protocols (e.g. cross validation for IID/non-IID splits, multiple seeds for stability) clearing the path for empirical validation in various ways e.g. different attack levels, privacy budgets and device profiles . Through this operationalization rigid assessments of the trade-offs between the utility of the models and the difficulty of actual mobile deployments is possible with various federated recommendation strategies.

This chapter is an account of the whole experiment design with which we estimated the trade-offs between accuracy and privacy in federated learning particularly in their application to movie recommendation systems that are supposed to be run on mobile devices. To address three key research questions, which we formulated, our experiments will be aimed at. The first research question is RQ1 that investigates the impact of the different budgets of privacy on the quality of the recommendations, that is, whether the quality of recommendations gets impacted negatively or influenced one way or the other depending on the variation of privacy budgets. The second research question is based on RQ2 which would be concerned with the effectiveness of the privacy attacks since we primordially vary and change the settings of the privacy settings whether the privacy attacks will be maintained or the privacy attacks break when we do the varied settings of the privacy levels. And the third of the research questions that is RQ3 is the influence of data heterogeneity on the federated learning performance because in reality not all users watch the same type of movies or with preferences like in the homogeneous or IID case, the data is not homogenous, but non-uniform or non-IID data that may affect the federated learning performance

greatly. It costs us much next to bring us to the experimental pipeline. It contains the data preparation, contains the non-IID data splitting and this is the way we cut the data into fragments that model real users and contains the in memory simulated federated learning, the other is the addition of the privacy and this is where we literally add the privacy operations, the third one is the privacy attack measurement, and the last one is the overall measure gathering. Other than all this, there was also companion mobile client that was developed by Flutter also there to illustrate the fact that it is not a simulation or a theoretical discussion but a real application that can be deployed and used by an actual mobile device.

These are all the experiments which are being run on a single work station on multi-core CPU. It does not imply any GPU acceleration, and even the size of the model (approximately 168K parameters) and the size of the dataset (100K interactions) are relatively small and render it possible to be trained on a computer. The entire series of the 30 experiments may require the use of 2.53 -hours wall-clock time. The full usage of the main memory can be about 4 GB RAM as it is used to execute the whole experiment, and the artefacts of the results (JSON, CSV, and PNG figures) occupy less than 500 MB of disk Space.

The python code in the server-side is fully python written code. The main depth-learning framework that is used to explain the model, run forward/back pass and the control of the gradient of DP-SGD is PyTorch. NumPy similarly is applicable in partitioning of data by Dirichlet distribution and general numeric operations. Pandas is used in the loading and aggregation of tabular data. The membership inference attack and popular evaluations tools make use of the Random Forest classifier at Scikit-learn. All figures are created and are publisable by matplotlib and Seaborn. Implementation of the optional server, which is founded on a Web service, like HTTP is done through FastAPI that will be deployed on distributed and mobile clients.

The MovieLens 100K data is imported to CSV format having four variables user id, movie id, rating, and time. The user and item identifiers are re-mapped to direct ones as integers with zero indices in the embedding layers with the index denoting the user or item identifier. Following the re-map process, the set of distinct users is 943, distinct items are 1,682 and records of the interactions in the dataset are 100 000. In order to form a binary rating in the regression based evaluation pipeline, because it is in a continuous form, the ratings are not binarized; on the binary
classification variant which is adopted in some attack evaluation, the regression based evaluation pipeline is left continuous and binarized with a rating threshold of 4.0.
The two blocks of 100,000 interactions are divided with 80,000 interaction training

, random shuffled. It is split at the interaction level and not user level in which all users are guaranteed of having a small number of ratings on both test and training set. This enables one to make an approximation of per-user recommendation measures on the test set.
4.3.3 Non-IID Data Partitioning via Dirichlet Distribution
To simulate realistic mobile data distributions where each device holds a distinct and non-overlapping subset of user interactions, the 80,000 training interactions are partitioned among
= 100 simulated clients using a Dirichlet distribution with concentration parameter
Interactions are grouped by user ID, yielding up to 943 groups.
For each user, a probability vector
represents the fraction of that user’s interactions assigned to client
Each interaction is independently assigned to a client according to the sampled proportions. This introduces controlled heterogeneity: a small
concentrates most of a user’s data on one or two clients, while a larger
The resulting partition is inherently imbalanced. With

(the default), client dataset sizes range from roughly 100 to 1,500 interactions, with a mean of 800. This imbalance is representative of production mobile deployments where user activity levels vary widely.
Most clients receive data from 1–2 users only
Clients receive data from several users with overlap
Data distributed more uniformly, approaching IID

The federated learning experiments are applied on the basis of the in-memory simulation that renders the in-memory variation of the Federated Averaging (FedAvg) protocol without introducing network latency and serialization expenses. In each communication round every client loads a deep copy of the current state dictionary of the global model, performs the local training on its own data partition and transmits the resulting state dictionary to the server. The server will then do weighted average (Section 3.4 of the Methodology) in order to arrive at the next global model. The design permits this complete 30-experiment suite to equally execute on a single machine and further act functionally identical to a distributed deployment.
Each federated learning round executes the following steps:
The state dictionary of the model distributed around the world is copied to the 100 clients. The new Matrix
Factorization module is created and obtained parameters are loaded in each client
All clients are trained with its local data component on
local epochs using SGD (learning rate 0.01, weight decay

) using a batch size of 32. The Loss of the regression variant is the Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) of the binary variant. When DP is employed, graduate clipping and noise injection follows a step of DP-SGD.
Parameters of the current state dictionary and the local number of samples of each client are acquired.

The Held-out test set is the test set on which the global model is tested. At the key checkpoints (rounds 1, 5 and 10), a full assessment ( each with recommendation metrics ) is carried out to reduce the computational load, and only simple regression metrics (MSE, MAE) are made round-by-round.
Round-level measurements are recorded and sent to an in-memory logger and then written as logs on disk as either a JSON or CSV file at the end of the experiment.
Differential privacy is enforced at the gradient level during local client training. After each backward pass, two operations are applied sequentially:
The L2 norm of the full gradient vector (concatenated across all model parameters) is computed. If the norm exceeds the clipping threshold
, all per-parameter gradients are scaled down by the factor.
This bounds the maximum contribution of any single training sample to the model update.
After clipping, independent Gaussian noise drawn from
) is added to each parameter’s gradient. The noise multiplier
is pre-computed to achieve the target privacy budget
given the number of training steps, batch size, and dataset size.
The clipping and noise operations are applied at every gradient step within the local training loop, ensuring that each mini-batch update satisfies the local DP guarantee.
RDP accountant will be in charge of tracking cumulative loss of privacy of all round.
is computed using the Rényi Differential Privacy (RDP) accountant. The accountant evaluates the RDP guarantee at a range of Rényi orders
{1.1, 1.2, …, 1.9, 2, 3, …, 64} and converts the tightest bound to (
. The key parameters for the accounting are:
Total RDP = 750 × per-step RDP (linear composition under RDP)
Table 5: Differential Privacy Configurations and Achieved Guarantees
The membership inference attack (MIA) follows the shadow-model paradigm described in Section 3.7.1 of the Methodology. The attack is configured as follows:
Three shadow models are trained, each on a random subset of the training data containing up to
. Each shadow model is trained for 5 epochs with the same optimizer configuration as the target model (SGD, lr = 0.01, weight

For each sample presented to a shadow model, the following features are extracted: (1) the model’s prediction value, (2) the absolute prediction error, (3) the L2 norm of the user embedding, (4) the L2 norm of the item embedding, (5) the dot product of the user and item embeddings, and (6) the full user and item embedding vectors (flattened). This yields a feature vector of dimension 5 + 2×64 = 133.

A Random Forest classifier with 50 estimators (scikit-learn defaults otherwise) is trained on the labeled feature vectors from all three shadow models. The classifier learns to discriminate between member (training) and non-member (test) samples.

The trained classifier is evaluated on 100 member and 100 non-member samples drawn from the target model. Performance is measured by AUC (area under the ROC curve) and classification accuracy. An AUC of 0.5 corresponds to random guessing (no privacy leakage).
The model inversion attack attempts to reconstruct a user’s top-
preferred items from the trained model. For each of 50 randomly sampled target users:
• All 1,682 items are scored using the model’s prediction function.
• The top-10 items by predicted score are selected as the “reconstructed” preferences.
• These are compared against the ground-truth top-10 items (determined by actual training ratings).
• A user is considered “successfully attacked” if at least 20% (i.e., 2 out of 10) of the reconstructed items overlap with the true top-10.
The attack success rate is reported as the fraction of the 50 target users for whom the overlap threshold is met.

The experimental design consists of four experiment groups, totaling 30 individual experiment runs. Each federated learning configuration is repeated with three random seeds (42, 123, 456) to report mean ±standard deviation. The following subsections describe each group.

A centralized model is trained on the full 80,000-interaction training set for 50 epochs with batch size 64, learning rate 0.01, and no differential privacy. This configuration serves as the upper bound for recommendation accuracy and provides the reference point against which federated learning degradation is measured. The centralized model uses the same
architecture with embedding dimension 64, totaling 167,936 parameters.
Federated learning is run with 100 clients, 10 communication rounds, and
, 8, 4, 2, 1}. Each of the five configurations is repeated with three seeds, yielding 15 experiments. The

configuration disables DP-SGD entirely (no clipping, no noise). This experiment group isolates the effect of differential privacy on recommendation quality while holding the data distribution constant.

For each of the five DP budgets in Experiment 2 (using seed 42), the trained model is subjected to both the membership inference attack and the model inversion attack. This yields five attack evaluation configurations. The attacks are applied to the final global model after round 10. This experiment group assesses whether increasing the DP guarantee effectively reduces the success rate of practical privacy attacks.
Federated learning is run with 100 clients, 10 rounds, no differential privacy (
{0.1, 0.5, 1.0}. Each configuration is repeated with three seeds, yielding 9 experiments (of which 3 overlap with the DP sweep at
). This experiment group isolates the effect of data heterogeneity on federated learning performance by removing the confound of differential privacy.
Table 6: Summary of All Experiment Configurations

Table 7 consolidates all hyperparameters used across the experimental suite. Values were selected based on standard practices in the federated learning and recommendation systems literature and kept constant across all experiments unless explicitly noted as a swept variable.

Recommendation metrics are computed using the full item catalog scoring approach. For each sampled test user (up to 100 users), the model scores all 1,682 items by computing the dot product of the user’s embedding with every item embedding. Items are ranked by predicted score in descending order, and the top-10 items are selected. The following metrics are then computed:
uses the original 1–5 ratings as graded relevance scores.
, normalized by the ideal DCG (IDCG) obtained from a perfect ranking of the user’s items.
is a binary indicator: 1 if any item in the top-10 has a ground-truth rating
4.0, and 0 otherwise. The average across users gives the hit rate.
is the fraction of items in the top-10 with a ground-truth rating
is the fraction of all items with a ground-truth rating
Recommendation metrics are computed at key checkpoint rounds (1, 5, and 10) rather than every round, as scoring all items for all users is computationally more expensive than the regression metrics.

MSE and MAE are computed over the full 20,000-interaction test set every round. These metrics evaluate the model’s ability to predict exact rating values rather than its ranking performance, providing a complementary view of model quality.
Three privacy metrics are collected for each attack evaluation:
Area under the ROC curve for the membership inference classifier. Values near 0.5 indicate that the classifier performs no better than random, meaning the model does not leak membership information.
Classification accuracy of the attack classifier on the balanced evaluation set (100 members, 100 non-members). Baseline accuracy is 0.5.
Fraction of target users whose top-10 preferences were successfully reconstructed with
Each experiment produces two output files:

A comprehensive record containing the experiment identifier, timestamp, full configuration dictionary, per-round metrics (train loss, test MSE, MAE, and recommendation metrics at checkpoint rounds), aggregation statistics (number of participating clients, total samples), and final metrics.

A tabular summary with one row per round, suitable for quick inspection and import into spreadsheet tools. Columns include round number, train loss, all six evaluation metrics, and a final summary row.
Attack evaluation results are consolidated into a single JSON file (
) containing MIA AUC, MIA accuracy, and inversion accuracy for each

All figures are rendered at 1200×800 pixel resolution using Seaborn’s darkgrid style, with clear legends, axis labels, and error bars. Figures are saved as PNG files in the

companion mobile application built with Flutter (Dart) demonstrates the feasibility of on-device federated learning. The application targets Android (minimum API level 21) and includes the following components

A pure-Dart implementation of the same matrix factorization architecture used in the Python experiments. The model performs forward passes, computes gradients via manual backpropagation, and applies SGD updates entirely on-device without any native ML framework dependency.

Manages the federated learning lifecycle: registration with the server, downloading global model parameters, local training, and uploading updated parameters. Communication uses HTTP with JSON payloads; model parameters are serialized as base64-encoded Float32 arrays for cross-platform compatibility.
Tracks battery level, battery drain rate, memory usage, and device information during training. These metrics quantify the on-device computational cost of federated learning.

A single-screen interface displaying the server URL input, connection status, training logs (last 50 messages), per-round metrics, and resource consumption. Users can initiate training and export results.
The distributed deployment uses a FastAPI server that exposes the following endpoints:
— Client registration with a unique client ID.
— Server-side model initialization with specified dimensions (num_users, num_items, embedding_dim).
— Download current global model parameters as base64-encoded tensors.
— Upload local model updates along with the client’s sample count for weighted aggregation.

Model parameters are transmitted as JSON objects containing the parameter name, tensor shape, data type, and base64-encoded Float32 byte arrays. For the 167,936-parameter model, a single parameter upload is approximately 650 KB.
Several measures ensure full reproducibility of all reported results:

All stochastic operations—PyTorch weight initialization, NumPy Dirichlet sampling, data shuffling, and train/test splitting—use fixed seeds. Each experiment configuration is run with seeds {42, 123, 456}, and results are reported as mean ± standard deviation.
The train–test split uses a fixed permutation, and the Dirichlet partitioning is seeded, so identical client data partitions are produced across runs with the same seed.
The complete experiment pipeline—data loading, partitioning, training, evaluation, and analysis—is encapsulated in
, which can be executed end-to-end without manual intervention.
Every experiment writes a self-contained JSON file with the full configuration and all per-round metrics, enabling independent verification and re-analysis.
All code, configuration, data processing scripts, and result files are maintained in a Git repository.

The experimental setup provides a controlled, reproducible, and comprehensive framework for investigating accuracy–privacy trade-offs in federated learning for mobile recommendation. The design systematically varies the privacy budget
(RQ1), evaluates privacy attacks across all DP configurations (RQ2), and examines data heterogeneity via the Dirichlet parameter

(RQ3). A total of 30 experiment runs—spanning 1 centralized baseline, 15 DP sweep configurations, 5 attack evaluations, and 9 heterogeneity sweep configurations—are executed with triple-seed averaging to ensure statistical reliability. The in-memory simulation architecture enables rapid iteration while remaining functionally equivalent to a real distributed deployment, which is demonstrated by the companion Flutter mobile client.

This chapter presents the results of the 30 experiments conducted to evaluate the accuracy–privacy trade-offs in federated learning for mobile movie recommendation. The results are organized around the three research questions: Section 5.2 establishes the centralized baseline; Section 5.3 examines how the differential privacy budget

affects recommendation quality (RQ1); Section 5.4 evaluates the effectiveness of privacy attacks under varying DP configurations (RQ2); and Section 5.5 analyzes the impact of data heterogeneity on federated learning performance (RQ3). Section 5.6 provides a cross-cutting discussion of the key findings. All federated results report the mean ± standard deviation across three random seeds (42, 123, 456) unless otherwise noted.
The centralized model, trained on the full 80,000-interaction training set for 50 epochs, serves as the upper bound for recommendation accuracy. Table 8 presents the final metrics at epoch 50.
The centralized model achieves an NDCG@10 of 0.225 and a Hit@10 of 0.38, meaning that 38% of test users receive at least one relevant recommendation (rating

4.0) in their top-10 list. The training loss converges from 13.74 at epoch 1 to 1.87 at epoch 50, demonstrating that the matrix factorization model effectively learns the user–item interaction patterns in a centralized setting.

Notably, the best recommendation metrics are observed at epoch 30 (NDCG@10 = 0.284, Hit@10 = 0.46) rather than epoch 50, indicating mild overfitting in the later training stages. The MSE decreases monotonically (from 13.62 at epoch 10 to 2.05 at epoch 50), suggesting that while the model continues to improve its rating predictions, the ranking quality degrades slightly due to overfitting to the training distribution. These centralized results establish the reference point against which all federated learning experiments are compared.
5.3 RQ1: Impact of Differential Privacy on Recommendation Accuracy
Table 9 presents the recommendation quality metrics across all five DP budget configurations. All experiments use 100 clients, 10 communication rounds, and
Results are averaged over three seeds.
Table 9: Recommendation Quality vs. Differential Privacy Budget (mean ± std, 3 seeds)

The results reveal a clear, monotonic accuracy–privacy trade-off. As the privacy budget ε decreases from ∞(no privacy) to 1 (very strong privacy), NDCG@10 declines from 0.0539 to 0.0467, representing a 13.4% relative degradation. Hit@10 follows a similar pattern, dropping from 6.33% to 5.33% (a 15.8% relative decrease). These trends are visualized in Figure 15.

The convergence plots reveal striking differences in training dynamics across privacy budgets. The no-DP configuration (
= 8) maintain relatively stable training losses around 13.7 and 32–38 respectively. In contrast, the strong-privacy configurations (
= 1) exhibit steadily increasing training losses across rounds, reaching 4,390 and 50,548 by round 10. This divergence indicates that the noise magnitude at
2 exceeds the gradient signal, causing the model parameters to drift rather than converge.

The NDCG@10 convergence panel shows that recommendation quality is measured at rounds 1, 5, and 10. All configurations exhibit similar NDCG@10 values at the measured checkpoints (ranging from 0.04 to 0.065), despite the vastly different loss magnitudes. This finding confirms
that the ranking metric is more robust to DP noise than the regression metrics, as the relative ordering of item predictions is partially
preserved even when absolute values are perturbed.
Table 12 presents the results of the shadow-model-based membership inference attack (MIA) across all five DP configurations. The attack is evaluated on the final global model from seed 42.
The membership inference attack is remarkably ineffective across all configurations, even without differential privacy. At

(no DP), the MIA achieves an AUC of only 0.548 and accuracy of 54.5%, barely exceeding the random baseline of 0.5. This indicates that the federated learning process itself provides a degree of inherent privacy protection: because each client’s data only influences the global model indirectly through aggregation, the model does not memorize individual training samples as strongly as a centralized model would.
= 4), the MIA AUC drops to 0.502, which is statistically indistinguishable from random guessing. At

= 1, the AUC falls to 0.486, slightly below 0.5, meaning the attack classifier performs worse than random. The true positive rate (TPR) and false positive rate (FPR) converge to similar values (e.g., TPR = 0.64, FPR = 0.64 at
= 2), confirming that the classifier cannot distinguish members from non-members.
Table 13 presents the model inversion attack results, which attempt to reconstruct each user’s top-10 preferred items from the trained model.

The model inversion attack fails completely across all configurations, achieving a 0% success rate for all 50 target users at every privacy level. Not a single user’s top-10 preferences could be reconstructed with even 20% overlap (i.e., 2 out of 10 items). This result holds even at

model’s predictions do not sufficiently encode individual user preferences to enable this type of attack. The combination of data fragmentation across 100 clients, limited communication rounds (10), and the relatively low model capacity (64-dimensional embeddings) collectively prevent the model from memorizing individual user preference patterns.

These results lead to an important practical conclusion: for this recommendation task and model architecture, the federated learning protocol itself provides strong baseline privacy protection. Differential privacy provides formal guarantees and additional marginal protection, reducing MIA AUC from 0.548 to 0.500, but the practical privacy risk is already minimal without DP.

w:r.w:t: Figure 12: NDCG@10 and Hit@10 as a function of data heterogeneity (α). Error bars show ±1 standard deviation across 3 seeds. No differential privacy is applied (ε = ∞).

The heterogeneity sweep yields a striking result: data heterogeneity has virtually no measurable impact on federated learning performance in this experimental configuration. NDCG@10 remains constant at 0.054 and Hit@10 at 0.063 across all three α values. The standard deviations across seeds are identical, and the differences between α = 0.1 and α = 1.0 are within the fourth decimal place. As shown in Figure 12, the error bars overlap almost entirely.
Several factors explain the observed robustness to data heterogeneity:

All 100 clients participate in every round. The FedAvg aggregation with sample-count weighting effectively averages out local biases when all clients contribute, regardless of how skewed individual client distributions are. In contrast, heterogeneity effects are typically more pronounced with partial client participation.

With only 10 communication rounds, the model has not converged sufficiently for heterogeneity-induced client drift to manifest. Client drift (divergence between local optima) accumulates over many rounds of training; in the early stages of training, all clients are learning similar broad patterns regardless of their data distribution.

The matrix factorization model with 64-dimensional embeddings has limited capacity to overfit to individual client distributions. A more complex model (e.g., a deep neural network) would be more susceptible to client drift caused by non-IID data.

With 80,000 interactions distributed across 100 clients (average 800 per client), the per-client data is too sparse for local models to develop strongly divergent update directions, especially within 3 local epochs.
To provide context for the federated learning results, Figure 13 visualizes the distribution of training samples across clients and Figure 14 shows the aggregation statistics across rounds.

w:r.w:t: Figure 15: Recommendation metrics (Hit@10, Precision@10, Recall@10) tracked over 10 training rounds for the no-DP federated configuration.

Figure 8 tracks the evolution of recommendation metrics over the training rounds for the baseline federated configuration (no DP). Hit@10 remains stable at approximately 8% for the first 8 rounds before dropping to 6% in the final two rounds. Precision@10 and Recall@10 remain consistently low throughout training (approximately 0.8% and 0.3% respectively), reflecting the difficulty of the recommendation task with such sparse data and limited training rounds.

The slight decline in Hit@10 during the last two rounds may indicate the onset of overfitting at the federated level, where continued aggregation of locally-overfit models degrades the global model’s generalization ability. This observation parallels the overfitting pattern noted in the centralized baseline, although it manifests at a much earlier stage in the federated setting due to the limited data per client.
The most significant finding across all experiments is the substantial performance gap between centralized and federated learning. Table 15 summarizes this gap.
Table 15: Centralized vs. Federated Performance Comparison

The federated model retains only 24% of the centralized model’s NDCG@10 and 17% of its Hit@10. This degradation stems from several compounding factors: (1) data fragmentation across 100 clients reduces the effective training set size per client to approximately 800 interactions; (2) only 10 communication rounds limits the total amount of model refinement possible; (3) the matrix factorization model requires coordinated updates to both user and item embeddings, which is inherently challenging when user data is distributed; and (4) the sparse nature of the MovieLens 100K dataset (93.7% sparsity) exacerbates the data scarcity problem at the client level.
When the analysis is restricted to the federated regime, the DP-induced accuracy loss is moderate and follows a predictable pattern. Table 16 isolates the incremental impact of DP by comparing each
configuration against the no-DP federated baseline.
= 8 offers the best privacy–accuracy trade-off for this task: it reduces MIA AUC from 0.548 to 0.534 (a 2.6% improvement in privacy protection) while incurring only a 0.9% accuracy loss. At
= 4, the MIA AUC reaches 0.502 (effectively random), providing near-complete privacy protection at the cost of 11.1% NDCG@10 degradation. Configurations with
2 offer diminishing returns: the MIA is already at random performance at
= 4, so further tightening the privacy budget yields no additional practical privacy benefit while continuing to erode accuracy.
The experimental results yield several practical implications for deploying federated recommendation systems on mobile devices:
= 4–8 renders membership inference attacks completely ineffective while preserving most of the federated model’s accuracy. Very strong privacy (
= 1) offers no additional practical protection and is therefore unnecessary for this use case.
Even without DP, the federated aggregation process limits privacy leakage to marginal levels (MIA AUC = 0.548). Model inversion attacks are completely
ineffective at all privacy levels, indicating that individual user preferences cannot be reconstructed from the federated model.

The 76% accuracy gap between centralized and federated training indicates that 10 communication rounds are insufficient for convergence. Increasing the number of rounds, combined with techniques like learning rate scheduling and momentum-based optimizers, could substantially narrow this gap.

The FedAvg algorithm with full client participation is robust to data heterogeneity in this configuration, suggesting that non-IID data distributions are not a primary concern for mobile recommendation deployment.

In summary, the results demonstrate that federated learning for mobile recommendation faces a significant accuracy challenge compared to centralized training, but this gap is primarily attributable to the limited communication budget and data fragmentation—not to differential privacy. DP at moderate levels (

= 4–8) provides robust formal privacy guarantees while imposing only a small additional accuracy penalty. Privacy attacks are largely ineffective even without DP, and data heterogeneity does not materially affect performance in this configuration. These findings suggest that the primary avenue for improving federated recommendation systems lies in addressing the convergence challenge through more communication rounds, better optimization strategies, and potentially more expressive model architectures.

This research empirically evaluated the trade-offs between utility and privacy in federated recommendation systems using the MovieLens 100K dataset. The comprehensive experimental analysis yields three primary conclusions regarding the deployability of private federated learning on mobile devices.

First, differential privacy proves to be a viable protection mechanism that does not prohibitively sacrifice utility. The results demonstrate that a privacy budget in the range of epsilon in [4, 8] offers an optimal trade-off, providing rigorous formal privacy guarantees while maintaining recommendation accuracy comparable to non-private baselines. Stricter privacy settings (lower epsilon) result in a sharp utility drop, identifying this range as the critical operating point for practical deployment.

Second, the vulnerability analysis reveals that the sparsity of recommendation data acts as a natural privacy defense. Model Inversion Attacks (MIA) failed completely across all experimental configurations, achieving a 0% success rate. Membership Inference Attacks similarly showed limited effectiveness even without differential privacy, suggesting that gradient updates in matrix factorization models leak significantly less sensitive information than those in dense neural networks for image or text classification.

Third, the system demonstrated unexpected robustness to data heterogeneity. Contrasting with common challenges in federated learning, variations in the non-IID degree (controlled by the Dirichlet parameter alpha) had negligible impact on model convergence and final accuracy. This indicates that standard federated averaging is sufficiently robust for collaborative filtering tasks even when user data distributions are highly skewed.

While privacy and heterogeneity concerns were effectively mitigated, the study identified that the primary performance bottleneck is the limited communication budget. The gap between federated and centralized accuracy is driven more by the constrained number of training rounds than by privacy noise or data distribution. Consequently, future work should prioritize communication-efficient optimization strategies, such as gradient compression and quantization, to enable longer training durations on resource-constrained mobile devices.

Additionally, while the current attack models were ineffective, future research should explore more sophisticated threats tailored to sparse data, such as property inference attacks, to ensure comprehensive security in production environments.

Future research efforts will aim at making the model more robust to a larger class of adversarial threats that are present in federated learning environments. This includes model poisoning attacks, in which malicious clients send data updates to other clients that are invalid or not converged to disrupt

the global model convergence and accuracy, attacks against membership inference, which uses model output or gradient to learn user preference on private information, which is used in personalized recommendation systems, such as personal movie watching history, and attacks against model inversion to recover private data trained by the model. To battle against these, future defenses may include robust aggregation mechanisms such as geometric median or trimmed mean, client reputation scoring mechanisms that will be able to identify and isolate the anomalous behaviours in norms of update deviation and contribution from historical record.

Strengthening of privacy mechanisms remain equally important, especially, with respect to complex and heterogeneous network environments (where the distribution of data is not IID in nature) (e.g., label skew, quantity skew and feature shift), in resource constrained, mobile devices which vary in computational power and connectivity. Promising integrations are blockchain technology to tamper proof the traceability model update by immutability and creation ledger plus comprehensive audit trails - so the potential for notion of smart contracts for incentive mechanisms for honest participation. Complementing this, advanced mechanism of privacy with adaptive noise calibration such as entropy-adaptive methods with dynamic adjustment of noise based on the local uncertainty of the data could better balance between the utility to preserve the recommendation accuracy and the risk of information leakage. Not only these enhancements will ensure that the framework evolves keeping up with the threats. strict rules such as GDPR, CCPA etc. while scaling up to large scale deployments will be complied.

A major direction for progress is to look for homomorphic encryption schemes that are more different from partially homomorphic encryption (e.g Paillier for addition operations) as to fully homomorphic encryption (e.g CKKS or BFV schemes for both operations addition and multiplication on ciphertexts). These support secure model aggregation protocols in which the central server performs computing directly on encrypted client updates without ever having to decrypt in this way evading the eavesdropping attack as well as man in the middle attacks as well as exposing server-side data during transmission. Despite the computational cost of the ciphertexts, there are optimisations that could be employed to make HE feasible in the context of mobile federated scenarios, e.g. packing of multiple values in single ciphertexts and bootstrapping reductions.

Complementing HE, advanced client selection algorithms, which includes adaptive strategies for adapting with the actual-time network conditions, contribution-based strategies for evaluating the quality of the gradient and the nom similarity, fairness-aware strategy for ensuring a fair representation in user demographics, and grouping strategies based on clustering clients depending on the data distribution similarity is capable of dynamically prioritizing participants. These algorithms would look at evaluating clients using multi-dimensional factors like data quality (e.g. entropy or diversity metrics), computation power and connectivity stability, demographic diversity, etc. to optimise the accuracy vs. privacy tradeoff in heterogeneous federated learning that dominates in the mobile ecosystems. Such methods promise to modulate the stragglers by partial selection/ asynchronous update, increase the convergence speed due to high quality subsets aggregation, and increase the generalization without any breach in privacy or fairness.

Furthermore, the promising breakthrough of the server-less architectures based on fully decentralized networks, and their use of blockchain consensus mechanisms (e.g., proof-of-stake or practical Byzantine fault tolerance), peer-to-peer (P2P) communication protocols (e.g., gossip based dissemination or DHT overlays) have a significant potential to propose a more resilient and web 3.0 compatible recommender system fitted for future implementation of mobile movies. Unlike the

conventional, federated learning architecture that requires a centralized server in order to coordinate the training, these are all server-less federated scenarios where all the features of coordination are distributed across the participating nodes and blockchain technology's unchangeable distributed ledgers are used to permanentlylog the updating of the results and smart contacts in order to automate the distribution of the reward based on the quality of the contribution. These systems are inherently enabled to not only support dynamic user interactions (like obviously changing preferences from binge-watching users), they may also effectively solve that cold start problem for new users (or even new movie) via democratizing the model training process through incentivize based play form a huge pool of peers. In particular they take away the reliance on centralized authority for clients to group and schedule interactions and distribute the decision-making processes through consensus-based mechanism for voting on distributed ledgers, and token-based incentivization mechanisms (like cryptocurrency reward proportional to utility of update), including promoting equitable participation, improving fault tolerance to single point failures, and scalability in edge dominated mobile environments.

Emerging directions also cope with federated continual learning frameworks - in which models may progressively adapt to changing user tastes in streaming situations without falling prey of catastrophic forgetting of prior know-how - which can be achieved by techniques such as elastic weight consolidation or replay buffer for non-IID federated streaming. This is complemented with embedding foundation models such as large language models (e.g. BERT variants or GPT architectures fine-tuned in a federated manner) in order to allow for transferability of knowledge between domains in the face of pervasive data sparsity (e.g. in niches of movie genres or in terms of emerging user demographics). Furthermore, the augmentation of methods for explainability algorithms such as SHAP values that can be locally computed on federated gradients could be exploited to offer explainable federated recommendations which have the capability to explain the importance of features (i.e. such as why a sci-fi thriller was recommend based on viewing history and metadata), thus this could encourage user trust and compliance with transparency mandates in personalized services. Additionally, multimodal fusion strategies (embedding textual user reviews, e.g. via sentiment analysis, rich metadata, e.g. genres, e.g. directors, cast and contextual signals, e.g. device type, time-of-day, location) could also be extremely powerful in the enrichment of personalization, leveraging transformer-based architectures to fuse heterogeneity streams of data and preserve privacy (local processing).

Subsequent studies will deal with the question of holistic approaches for maintaining the trade-off between the communication efficiency, good privacy guarantees and computational overhead in resource-constrained scenarios. Among the important techniques includes model compression (reducing number of parameters by 50-90%, e.g. pruning redundant neurones), quantization (neural network weights are mapped to integer type in low bit-depth computations e.g. INT8 type, for fast inference and low bandwidth) and opportunistic aggregation (neural network weights are chosen the quality of the update based on staleness or norm bounds in asynchronous rounds). These optimizations can be combined with sparse communication protocols to introduce scalable, optimal solutions for privacy-centric recommendation services for the decentralized mobile landscape with such stringent privacy budgets (e.g. ), which sub-second latency would be the result even with high heterogeneity. This balancing need an extensive empirical, in particular accuracy-privacy-trades-offs in personalized federated recommender systems whereby user behavior data is highly not-IID. Specifically, in the future, the conditions of the dynamic network and the approaches toward stragglers' mitigation need to be adapted and re-tailored in the real-world, to ensure the simultaneous

guarantee of the system performance and data privacy. Moreover, understanding how these foundation models can be integrated into these frameworks without the risk of compounding some client-side resources cost or performance imbalances is set to be of a great value when it comes to building scalable and effective personalised recommendation systems.

It is a specific type of attack where the adversary can reverse engineer the original data just by looking at the series of gradient in the Federated learning system. Since the gradient have one vector of improving the error, the attacker knows in which direction to move so that the original data can be fetched.

The normal machine learning works by first fixing the data point and update the weights to minimise error in Deep leakage attack. The attacker fixes the weights and gradient and then works back by updating the dummy data to match the gradient.
In a neural network, the gradient with respect to the weights
is calculated by multiplying terms all the way back from the output to the input.

As an example, let’s see if we have a picture containing lots of pixels and we are to encode it with gradient algorithm, then it is to be noted that every pixel in the input influences the final gradient value. Therefore, we can conclude that the gradient codes, the pixel values.

Since every value in the chain of the chain rule is deterministic and perfect, leading to the initial input values. The only way to break this chain is to add some perturbation, or we can say noise to at least one of the values in between the chain rule.

The adversary is trying to track the original value using the chain rule in reverse. He will be encountered with this noise. This noise will prevent their anniversary from going into the right path back to the input in a sense, they are chasing a ghost. The mathematical mirror, which was helping the adversary to track back. The whole input value is no shattered, and he will be led to an image, which is meaningless or at least blurry in a worst case scenario.

In simple words, it is a “magic trick” that allows two people (an analogy) to share information while keeping their specific choices and secrets hidden from each other. It is a fundamental building block for almost all privacy preserving technologies today.

Take an example of two persons Alice and Bob. The sender in this case will be Alice and the recipient is Bob. Alice produces two envelopes one labelled zero and another one labelled the one and writes them down. Each envelope has a secret message which she writes as we shall call it Message 0 and Message 1 respectively. These messages are supposed to be confidential and one message over an envelope should be unique and the content of the mail should not be mixed and copied.

And now he, Bob, would give you the secret he wants, secret in one envelope, envelope number one, to be more precise. but he, time stands in a dilemma, he wants to see what the envelope number one contains, he wants to do what he got not, tell Alice his choice. Precisely,

Bob does not want to know anything other than what is in envelope one and does want to ensure that on his part there is as little information as possible at the mind of Alice as to the envelope chosen by Bob. It is a crucial necessity in which the privacy and secrecy of choice is essential and has some cryptographic protocols or secure voting systems.

On the other hand Alice too is concerned with privacy of her messages. She is collaborative to allow Bob a moment in order to read the only one of the two secret messages namely 0 and 1 than to not both of them. She does not want Bob to know whatever was in the other envelope whatever envelope he chooses. This would ensure that information that would otherwise be spread would be kept secret and there is no message that is left to flow into the hands of Bob than there is regarding his liking.

This kind of setup will give a delicate balance: Bob will not want to be discovered by Alice, and vice versa, neither is willing to make sure that Bob is only aware of one secret and is never allowed to utilize the other. It is not that straightforward and it is a special form of a protocol with a magic graciousness, allowing Bob to choose and learn privately one and only one message and for certain the choice that will be made in Alice will not be learned privately and the same message will not be learned either. This is the basic challenge that have made the Oblivious Transfer Protocol to be developed as has been explained below. Superior privacy protective technologies and secret multi-party procedures in the digital domain are based on such protocols.

If Bob requests envelope one then Alice knows his decision. If Alice sends them, both envelopes, then Bob will read both the messages. Hence in both of these case of the encryption the point of encryption is killed.

To elaborate, this scenario, brings two concern for privacy in a naive way of sharing secrets. Firstly if Bob asks Alice directly envelope one it is immediately obvious to Alice what message Bob is interested in. This eliminates the demand of privacy on the recipient, since his option is not hidden anymore from the sender. Bob's intention should be not public, it's prefer to him to communicate directly and the preference will be shown.

Secondly if Alice chooses to send both envelopes to Bob so that she does not know which he has chosen then unwittingly she gives Bob read access to both messages. This is violation to the privacy of the sender because Bob can now read both secrets and this is against the point of selective sharing. The basic issue in this is that either the individual sending the message knows what the receiver is slated to choose or the receiver finds out about all the secrets - in both the cases the fundamental principles behind encryption and privacy loses its meaning.

Therefore, a safe protocol is required where Bob is able to receive the only message (recieved) without the other message being exposed to Alice, and to Bob. This is the exact challenge that Oblivious Transfer Protocol is most designed to solve and ensure the privacy for both parties and doesn't leak out more information than it is meant to.

The Nadi Oblivious Transfer Protocol is a somewhat complex piece of mathematics, which is referred to as the public-key cryptography but the general logic of the process can be simply stated. We shall deconstruct and provide a description of each stage through basic analogy.

: Suppose that Alice assembles two types of digital locks (you can imagine them to be very secure padlocks), and she puts them on a table. She also possesses a master key which can unlock the two locks but most significantly she keeps the master key and so there should be no need to hand it over to Bob. That which Alice wants to guard, are these two locks, the two possible secrets or messages (Message 0 and Message 1) that Alice wants to ensure safety over.

: Now, Bob decides what secret to wish he could see, Lock 1 (say) in specific. however, he does not want Alice to know what was this very lock. In order to conceal his choice, he makes a special computational effort to cover the lock with blindness, he is able to fantasize that he can strip vegan lock or any substance on a lock of the door so that Alice would not know that he has picked the vegan lock. Then he uncovers this indolent eye (or symbol of his indolent decision) to Alice. This way, Alice is aware whether he is on the number Lock 0 or Lock 1.

: Alice gets signal by Bob/Blinded lock Because it is blind, Alice no means of telling what lock Bob is interested in. After this she transmits Message 0 and ciphers with the logic of Lock 0 only Lock 0 can decrypt and ciphers with the logic of Lock 1 Message 1. Then she can send back the two encrypted messages (the one encrypted using Lock 0 and one encrypted using Lock 1) to Bob.

: Bob has now been given the encrypted / locked messages. This implies that, courtesy of the entirely insane calculation he has made in the past (the blinding), he has the key logic of the lock to which he is interested, (viz., Lock 1). By this, he is able to pass the opening and reading of Message 1. When trying to open Message 0 though, it is not able to do so, the calculation is not working, the decryption is not working and he can only see gibberish or other non-sensible information. Bob then goes to take his message and not that of the other.

In summary, it is a protocol like a magic trick of digital locks and keys -bob is free to unlock any of the messages that he is not aware on which message he will unlock Alice and, after that, he is not able to unlock the other message. At the same time that the other half of the secret that Alice possesses has been preserved and she will never discover what message Bob was curious of. This comes with privacy to the receiver and message sender that is highly vital to cryptography and secure systems.

Now let's say we have a nuclear code or some kind of top secret code, That has to be kept safe in all cost. One solution is that we handover the code to one person who will keep it secret. But this solution is not very efficient, because if something happens to that person or if the person dies, the secret goes with him and the code is lost forever. Another solution is that sharing that same secret key with someone else. This creates a problem that upon sharing the secret, The risk of the secret getting known to the public gets doubled. Similarly, if we share the secret with number of people, The risk gets even bigger by that factor. So in other words, none of the above presented solutions work.

There is another solution. The key has to be. Divided among n number of trusted people. Now remember that these people are trusted and these people. Have a very low risk of Revealing the secret to the public. Also, none of the members of the group know the complete secret, so the secret remains secret. However, this solution is also not efficient. Because when it's time to reveal the secret, every one of the member has to be present at the same time. So if someone gets sick or if someone is busy somewhere and is not able to attend the meeting, the secret remains hidden.

This is where Shamir's secret sharing scheme comes to play. We want to distribute shares to people, such that any group of people can combine their shares to reveal the secret, but any group smaller than learns absolutely nothing. This scheme can be compared with privacy, in which one or 2 absence of data does not make much effect in the whole result of the system. So if at least number of people are present at the meeting, then number of people will have almost same result when all the shares are combined to reveal the secret.
